name: krb5-multidev
title: krb5-multidev
summary: development files for MIT Kerberos without Heimdal conflict
description:
development files for MIT Kerberos without Heimdal conflict
Kerberos is a system for authenticating users and services on a network.
Kerberos is a trusted third-party service.  That means that there is a
third party (the Kerberos server) that is trusted by all the entities on
the network (users and services, usually called "principals").
.
This is the MIT reference implementation of Kerberos V5.
.
Most users wishing to build applications against MIT Kerberos should
install libkrb5-dev.  However, that package conflicts with heimdal-dev.
This package installs libraries and headers in /usr/include/mit-krb5 and
/usr/lib/mit-krb5 and can be installed along side heimdal-multidev, which
provides the same facilities for Heimdal.

commands.name: krb5-config.mit
commands.description: tool for linking against MIT Kerberos libraries

arguments.description: prints the version, vendor, prefix, and exec-prefix.
arguments.raw: --all  prints the version, vendor, prefix, and exec-prefix.

arguments.description: prints the compilation flags used to build the Kerberos installation.
arguments.raw: --cflags  prints the compilation flags used to build the Kerberos installation.

arguments.description: tool for linking against MIT Kerberos libraries
arguments.raw: -config  tool for linking against MIT Kerberos libraries

arguments.description: prints the built-in default credentials cache location.
arguments.raw: --defccname  prints the built-in default credentials cache location.

arguments.description: prints the built-in default client (initiator) keytab location.
arguments.raw: --defcktname  prints the built-in default client (initiator) keytab location.

arguments.description: prints the built-in default keytab location.
arguments.raw: --defktname  prints the built-in default keytab location.

arguments.description: prints the prefix for executables for which the Kerberos installation was built.
arguments.raw: --exec-prefix  prints the prefix for executables for which the Kerberos installation was built.

arguments.description: prints a usage message. This is the default behavior when no options are specified.
arguments.raw: --help  prints a usage message. This is the default behavior when no options are specified.

arguments.description: prints the compiler options needed to link against library. Allowed values for library
arguments.raw: --libs  prints the compiler options needed to link against library. Allowed values for library

arguments.description: prints the prefix for which the Kerberos installation was built.
arguments.raw: --prefix  prints the prefix for which the Kerberos installation was built.

arguments.description: prints the name of the vendor of the Kerberos installation.
arguments.raw: --vendor  prints the name of the vendor of the Kerberos installation.

arguments.description: prints the version number of the Kerberos installation.
arguments.raw: --version  prints the version number of the Kerberos installation.

use_cases: When you need Kali Linux, use krb5-config.mit because development files for MIT Kerberos without Heimdal conflict.
When the engagement is in the install phase, use krb5-config.mit as a cataloged Kali Linux tool with operator ACCEPT/CANCEL gating.
When chaining Kali Linux tools, pair krb5-config.mit with sibling catalog entries sharing its attack_surface/phase_hint.
When validating lab setup, run `krb5-config.mit --help` (or the README entrypoint) and confirm required hardware/deps from the catalog.
